coredata.dc:title: Carbon nanomaterials with chitosan: A winning combination for drug delivery systems

coredata.dc:description: The design and synthesis of drug delivery systems (DDSs) with controlled release of pharmaceutical agents is essential to improve the treatment of cancer patients. So far, various types of DDSs encompassing nanomaterial scaffolds in combinations with organic, inorganic, and polymeric materials have been commercialized. In this field, chitosan and chitosan-modified polymers are the most considered natural biomaterials thank to their unique properties such as low cost, high viscosity, excellent biocompatibility, and biodegradability. In this review, recent reports on DDSs comprising chitosan/chitosan derivatives supported on carbon nanomaterials, along with their preparation and application, are evaluated. Special emphasis is put on multi-modal approaches to carbon nanomaterials, which made them particularly attractive for biomedical applications. Herein, recent advancement in functionalized carbon nanostructure comprising graphene oxide (GO), carbon nanotubes (CNTs), nanodiamond (ND), fullerene, and carbon quantum dots (CQDs) alongside chitosan/chitosan derivatives as drug vessels are discussed.

head.ce:title: Carbon nanomaterials with chitosan: A winning combination for drug delivery systems

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The design and synthesis of drug delivery systems (DDSs) with controlled release of pharmaceutical agents is essential to improve the treatment of cancer patients. So far, various types of DDSs encompassing nanomaterial scaffolds in combinations with organic, inorganic, and polymeric materials have been commercialized. In this field, chitosan and chitosan-modified polymers are the most considered natural biomaterials thank to their unique properties such as low cost, high viscosity, excellent biocompatibility, and biodegradability. In this review, recent reports on DDSs comprising chitosan/chitosan derivatives supported on carbon nanomaterials, along with their preparation and application, are evaluated. Special emphasis is put on multi-modal approaches to carbon nanomaterials, which made them particularly attractive for biomedical applications. Herein, recent advancement in functionalized carbon nanostructure comprising graphene oxide (GO), carbon nanotubes (CNTs), nanodiamond (ND), fullerene, and carbon quantum dots (CQDs) alongside chitosan/chitosan derivatives as drug vessels are discussed.

ce:section.ce:para:
To overcome the challenges associated with cancer treatment, considerable research effort has been invested in harnessing the beneficial attributes of nanotechnology. Nanodevices have found clinical applications in, for example, early detection of cancer and smart delivery of anti-cancer drugs. Due to their size, nanodevices can interact with biomolecules on the cell surface and inside the cells along with drug carriers that comprise dendrimers, micelles, liposomes, polymeric compounds, and nanoparticles [1,2] (see Schemes 1 and 2
)

A group of green and environmentally benign organic biopolymers that has attracted a lot of attention in recent years is the polysaccharides. Polysaccharides are naturally produced by living organisms through the repetitive addition of monosaccharides and contain cellulose, chitin/chitosan, starch, pectin, alginate, guar gum, xanthan gum, etc. Chitosan is the second most abundant biopolymer after cellulose, which is ideal for a wide range of applications [4] and is found in crustaceans namely lobsters, prawns, crabs, and shrimps and in the skeletons of insects and fungi, for example mucor and Aspergillus [5]) (Fig. 1 ).

Chitosan is relatively low-cost material and possesses unique properties such as high viscosity, and excellent biocompatibility and biodegradability [6]. It contains numerous hydroxyl (-OH) and amino (-NH2) groups that can establish coordination [7,8]. The cationic character of chitosan, due to its primary amino groups, makes it an inimitable bio-polymer for use in drug delivery systems [9] (Fig. 2 ).

Drug delivery systems based on chitosan have been known since the early 1990s and numerous studies have been reported on the potential biological applications of chitosan such as wound dressing [10], tissue engineering [11], slimming, and drug and gene delivery [12]. The toxicity of chitosan has also been studied in experimental and clinical trials; its safety and effectiveness are also indicated for perfusion [13–15]. The degree of deacetylation is the main factor in terms of chitosan's toxicity, a lower amount of deacetylation lead to lower toxicity [16]). The unique feature of chitosan is its biodegradation which also depends on the degree of deacetylation a higher degree causes much faster degradation [17]).

However, the presence of strong hydrogen bonds and the stable crystalline structure lead to chitosan's low solubility [18]. Moreover, its fast drug releases arises from its quick degradation, and the high swelling degree in aqueous environments limits its widespread applications. Consequently, physical and chemical changes are necessary to improve chitosan's stability for the preparation of sustained drug delivery systems.

Nanotechnology has immense potential, which has been reflected in our daily lives over the last decades. Undoubtedly, one of the most esteemed applications of nanomaterials is related to satisfactory drug delivery systems and medical treatments. In particular, the physicochemical properties of stable nano-chitosan make it a superb candidate for a variety of bio-applications. Recent strategies for modifying chitosan with nanomaterials have resulted in the generation of novel compounds with high porosity and large specific surface areas.

Although carbon nanomaterials (CNM) have been widely deployed for various bio-applications [19–22]; Zare et al., 2020; [23,24,2,25–28], they appear to be relatively less explored as drug carriers. Alongside their sustainable physicochemical properties, they are endowed with other excellent features such as high thermal and electrical conductivity, exceptional optical properties, and high stability [29,30]. The CNM group comprises graphene oxide (GO), carbon nanotubes (CNTs), carbon nanohorns (CNHs), nanodiamonds (ND), fullerene, and carbon quantum dots (CQDs). Graphene has a two-dimensional structure while carbon nanotubes are tubular hollow cylinders that appears to be constructed through rolling up graphene, and in the case of carbon nanohorns, graphene is rolled into a cone (Fig. 3 ).

Herein, this article sums up the recent developments in the nano-drug delivery systems using chitosan as a sustainable natural biopolymer in combination with the most attractive nanomaterials in recent years, carbon nanomaterials (CNM).

A vastly practical class of nanostructures comprises carbon nanostructures [31]. These nanomaterials can be applied as drug vessels and have been shown to possess a range of potential benefits; for instance, they can carry hydrophilic and hydrophobic materials, make site-specific delivery and maintain high drug stability [32–37]. CNTs are a type of nanostructure with a high aspect ratio and a variety of amazing features, including their size, high surface area, individual graphene-like configuration, and enormous mechanical stiffness [38]). In addition to the aforementioned features, they show promising chemical stability and electrical properties [39]; 2016). They exist as single-walled, double-walled, and multi-walled carbon nanotubes [40–44].

Zhang and co-workers reported a novel drug delivery system by supporting alginate and chitosan on a single-walled carbon nanotube (SWCNTs) for the controlled release of doxorubicin (DOX) [45]. Encapsulated SWCNTs attained by mixing alginate and chitosan were able to attach to and control the release of DOX. The final composite exhibited excellent stability under physiological conditions. However, in a tumor environment which has a reduced pH, DOX was efficiently released, culminating in cell death.

Ji and co-workers have described a highly effective targeted drug delivery system based on chitosan-wrapped SWNTs through a self-assembly process and then attached a targeting group of folic acid (FA) [46]. After characterization, its potential application as an anticancer drug (DOX) for tumor-targeting chemotherapy in SMMC-7721 liver cancer cell lines was investigated in vitro and in vivo.

A drug delivery system based on MWCNTs bed and chitosan was reported by Li and co-workers [47]. The tea polyphenol (TP) as a cancer ancillary drug was loaded onto this nanocomposite through hydrogen bonding, and the controlled drug release was accomplished by gamma radiation.

Chen and co-workers selected a drug delivery system applying an epidermal growth factor, carboxyl chitosan, and SWCNTs that were prepared through an ionic cross-linking process for loading and realizing the etoposide (ETO) [48]. The synthesized drug delivery system enhanced cell death on human alveolar carcinoma epithelial cell lines A549 (ATCC, CCL-185) as much as 2.7 times in comparison with free ETO in an in vitro test; the releasing mechanism for this system was determined by the pH conditions.

The modification of carboxyl derivatives of SWNTs with chitosan through a self-assembly process to form a drug delivery system has been reported by Mo and co-workers [49]. Hyaluronan (HA) was attached to this nanocomposite as a selective anti-cancer agent. The prepared system showed high drug-loading effectiveness and low cytotoxicity and side effects as it enabled controlled drug release depending on the pH conditions. HeLa cells were used to evaluate the therapeutic efficacy of this nanocomposite wherein higher toxicity was observed.

Al Shanqiti and co-workers [50] reported the modification of chitosan using hydroxyapatite, multiwalled carbon nanotubes, and glutaraldehyde via a simple blending and crosslinking procedure. They concluded that modified chitosan is more thermally stable than the unchanged chitosan while the blending of CNTs increased the thermal stability and its ability to bind to molecules.

Kumar and co-workers [51] functionalized CNTs with ultra-high molecular weight polyethylene. Subsequently, its surface was further modified with gentamicin loaded on chitosan through a chemical etching self-assembling technique, and this composite was applied as a continuous releasing agent for gentamicin; the antibacterial activity was evaluated by applying the Staphylococcus aureus bacteria cells.

Sharmeen and coworkers [52] prepared MWCNTs/gelatin-chitosan nanocomposite films through a solution casting process by using an ionic cross-linking method. They evaluated the improvement of physicomechanical and thermal properties of incorporated MWCNTs into the gelatin-chitosan. The ciprofloxacin, as an antibiotic drug, was loaded into the prepared MWCNTs/gelatin-chitosan nanocomposites, which showed effectiveness in drug release rate without any cytotoxicity.

Qi and coworkers [53] synthesized galactosylated chitosan via the reaction of lactobionic acid and chitosan using dialysis bags. The modified chitosan was conjugated to carbon nanomaterials via a self-assembling process. Next, the resulting composite was grafted to oxidized CNTs, and its performance for the pH-dependent sustained releasing of doxorubicin (DOX) was investigated; releasing was discerned under acidic conditions. The cytotoxicity of the synthesized nanocomposite was investigated through in vitro and in vivo experiments, and it displayed good activity against liver tumors in mice.

The poly(methacrylic acid) (PMAA) was coated with chitosan and MWCNTs via the self-assembly method [54]. The loading of gatifloxacin (GFLX) onto this nanohybrid was investigated and the in vitro experiments confirmed its high drug loading and sustained release properties.

Singh and co-workers [55] reported the conjugation of chitosan-folate to MWCNTs for the loading of docetaxel. The facile reaction of chitosan with folic acid in the presence of EDC as a linker agent was deployed to prepare chitosan-folate, which was tagged on the surface of MWCNTs through the self-assembly process. Both the biocompatibility and the safety of this nanocomposite were examined. The IC50 values revealed that this nanocomposite is effective, on an acceptable level in human lung cancer cells (A549 cells).

Saeednia and coworkers [56] prepared a thermosensitive hydrogel by using β-glycerophosphate, chitosan, and CNTs, and methotrexate was loaded onto this hybrid via a self-assembly process. The in vitro drug release study revealed that non-toxic CNT-containing hydrogels had a sufficient disrate charge and improved the antitumor function of methotrexate. The in vitro evaluation of the hybrid efficacy in delivering MTX on the cultured MCF-7 breast cancer cells revealed good results.

Single-walled carbon nanohorns (SWCNHs) have low toxicity and relatively uniform morphology. The preparation of SWCNHs entails a facile process using laser irradiation without the need for a metal catalyst. Moreover, their surface can be functionalized easily, thus making them special materials in biomedical applications [57–60]. Chen and coworkers reported the support of functionalized chitosan on the surface of SWCNHs; DOX was loaded onto SWCNHs through a
π − π
stacking interaction and further functionalized with modified chitosan to generate the final nanocomposite. Its efficiency as a chemotherapy agent under laser irradiation was evaluated in both in vitro and in vivo, with promising results being reported [61].

Oxidation and exfoliating reactions of graphite in aqueous media are deployed to generate graphene oxide (GO) by Hummers’ method [62]). The puckered sheets of GO can be exfoliated completely via the input of mechanical energy. During the oxidation of a basal plane, various functional groups such as epoxide, carboxyl, and hydroxyl are introduced on it, which makes it amenable to interactions with water and facilitates the exfoliation process while the layers individually are dispersed. These functional groups also make it water-soluble and improve its drug adsorption and mechanical strength [63,64]. Functionalized GO can be attached via a covalent bond to numerous drugs under mild conditions and can hold the drugs through nonbonding interaction such as hydrogen bonding and π–π interactions. The most well-thought-out drugs loaded on GO are doxorubicin (DOX) and camptothecin (CPT).

Recently, Jafari and co-workers [65] prepared a nanocomposite of chitosan-tripolyphosphate-graphene oxide via by simply mixing chitosan with tripolyphosphate and graphene oxide and the final nanocomposite was organized through an ionic cross-linking process. They investigated its performance for the controlled release of Sumatriptan Succinate (SS). The cytotoxicity and antimicrobial activity of the synthesized nanocomposite were investigated and the obtained results indicated biocompatibility and acceptable antimicrobial properties of the nanocomposite against bacteria.

Pan and co-workers [66] have reported the functionalization of graphene oxide (GO) with carboxymethyl chitosan and lactobionic acid through the self-assembly process and applied the ensuing nanocomposite to load doxorubicin (DOX) through an adsorption interaction, this hybrid exhibited release behavior as a pH-sensitive target. The synthesized nanocomposite showed high biocompatibility with liver cancer cell lines 8 SMMC-7721 and induced cell death after 24 h.

Anirudhan and co-workers [67] reported GO-based functionalized chitosan polyelectrolyte nanocomposite as a drug delivery system. In their reported technique, graphene oxide (GO) was changed initially to amine-functionalized GO (AGO); besides, chitosan was modified by folic acid (FA), itaconic acid, and acrylic acid monomers using linker agents such as N, N' -dicyclohexylcarbodiimide and ethylene glycol dimethacrylate, respectively. The chemically modified chitosan and amino graphene oxide were simply mixed to generate the complex. Next, doxorubicin (DOX) was loaded into the prepared nano-complex by π-π stacking interactions and hydrogen bonding interactions. The carrier exhibited excellent drug loading capacity and releasing properties on L929, HeLa and MCF7 cells.

Xie and coworkers [68] prepared magnetic nanoparticles of graphene oxide (mGO) through a simple loading method. The self-assembly method via a layer-by-layer tactic was used to functionalize the mGO with chitosan and sodium alginate. This nanocomposite was loaded with DOX, and its application was examined as an anti-cancer drug delivery system that indicated adequate efficiency. Human lung cancer cell lines (A549) were deployed for in vitro experiments which signified an excellent photothermal effect of the nanocomposite.

The hybrid of GO and galactosylated chitosan was synthesized through a non-covalent interaction and was evaluated by Wang and co-workers as a novel drug delivery system [69]. In this procedure, the loaded drug on galactosylated chitosan (GC) was tagged on a GO carrier through a non-covalent interaction. Its controlled drug-releasing ability at the tumor site was successful on HepG2 and SMMC-7721 cell lines as it displayed high tumor cytotoxicity, low normal physiological cytotoxicity, and efficient tumor growth inhibition.

Zhao and coworkers [70] prepared cationic chitosan-GO nanocomposite hydrogels that were applied for the loading of bovine serum albumin through electrostatic interactions. The release profiles of BSA from the hydrogel were revealed which almost followed the Fickian release behavior.

Chen and co-workers [71] reported the synthesis of hydrogel beads deploying chitosan, reduced graphene oxide (rGO), and alginate via a facile blending and heating technique, with the construction of the nanocomposite being based on self-assembly process. It was used as a novel carrier for pH-sensitive drug delivery systems and in MTT assay evaluation it exhibited low cytotoxicity to hepatic stellate cell lines.

In a report by Wang and co-workers [72], a hybrid of GO, carboxymethyl cellulose, and chitosan, was prepared by exploiting a calcium ion in the form of a crosslinker; therefore, the interaction of chitosan and GO in the final structure was based on ionic cross-linking. The resultant hybrid was successfully applied as the pH-responsive carrier for 5-fluorouracil (5-FU). Moreover, they studied the release kinetics of the hybrid aerogels based on the Higuchi and Korsmeyer-Peppas model which indicated that 5-FU release from the hybrid aerogels was controlled by Fickian diffusion.

Yang and co-workers [73] modified GO with carboxymethyl chitosan, fluorescein isothiocyanate, and hyaluronic acid via facile blending and sonication processes conjugated to each other via non-covalent interactions. The resulting nanocomposite was characterized and used to encapsulate doxorubicin (DOX) as an anticancer drug through π-π stacking interactions. The in vitro release behavior studies revealed that this drug carrier enabled a controllable release of DOX under acidic conditions. Studies into the cell uptake conducted on Hela cells and L929 cells indicated that the synthesized nanocomposite can transport DOX into Hela cells with enhanced toxicity.

Lei and co-workers [74] prepared a nanocarrier for anti-cancer drug loading using GO functionalized with chitosan and sodium alginate by an electrostatic self-assembly process. This nanocomposite exhibited high stability, solubility, and high loading capacity; DOX was loaded onto its surface and allowed excellent pH-sensitive drug delivery. The in vitro cytotoxicity assay showed that the DOX-loaded nanocomposite induced high cell toxicity against the MCF-7 cells.

Mahanta and co-workers [75] developed novel nanohybrid frameworks of chitosan using GO and sulfonated GO which were tagged together through a self-assembly process. The ensuing nanohybrid displayed a porous network structure with high hydrophilicity and improved mechanical strength. Nanohybrids can control the drug-release behavior and enable better biocompatibility in comparison with that of pure chitosan. The cytotoxicity of the nanocomposite was evaluated on the MG63 cell lines and proved its efficiency.

Zhao and co-workers [76] selected graphene oxide nanoparticles as the core of the drug carrier system and functionalized them with dimethyl maleic anhydride-modified chitosan, wherein the fabrication entailed the classic self-assembly of backbones by electrostatic interactions.

The doxorubicin hydrochloride (DOX) was adsorbed on the surface of the prepared nanocomposite via the π−π stacking interaction. This GO-based drug delivery system has various advantages such as brilliant encapsulation efficiency and high loading capacity. The in vitro investigations revealed that this hybrid presented the enhanced cellular uptake by HepG2 cells and tunable intracellular agent release profiles.

Deng and co-workers [77] designed a hybrid microcapsule (h-MC) containing polysaccharides, iron oxide, and GO. This nanohybrid was prepared by using a facile layer-by-layer method for coating chitosan, sodium alginate, and hyaluronic acid on a magnetic graphene oxide core. The drug loading in this nanocomposite allowed-controlling the pH control and the combination of three kinds of polysaccharides contributed to its high biocompatibility. The in vitro cytotoxicity studies performed on HeLa cells and the in vivo efficacy confirmed in HeLa-bearing BALC/nude mice indicated the effectiveness of the nanocomposite.

Wang and co-workers [78], provided an excellent platform for developing drug delivery systems for various types of cancer by using multi-functionalized graphene sheets, magnetic iron oxide nanoparticles, and chitosan which were gathered via the self-assembling process. The graphene sheets were made more stable, soluble, and biocompatible by the insertion of chitosan. The loading of DOX onto the nanocomposite efficiently enhanced the drug loading capacity. The nanocomposite was more active in killing A549 lung cancer cells than free DOX.

A non-covalent layer-by-layer self-assembly method was applied by Xie and co-workers [3]. to the formation of a nanocomposite involving GO, chitosan, and dextran functionalization for the anti-cancer drug delivery. This nanocomposite was deployed for the loading of doxorubicin (DOX) hydrochloride via electrostatic attraction with pH-sensitive DOX release patterns. Furthermore, they reported that the nanocomposite exhibited high cytotoxicity to the MCF-7 cancer cells.

A novel hybrid nanocarrier based on porous rGO and chitosan was designed by Hazhir and co-workers [79] using a self-assembly technique, and it was deployed for the loading of DOX via a hydrogen bonding interaction. The release rate properties of the nanocomposite under two different pH values were examined, with a faster release rate in an acidic environment being discerned.

Li and co-workers [80]) employed GO and chitosan to prepare a layer-by-layer nanocomposite on the SiO2–NH2 cores via a self-assembly process. Ibuprofen was loaded into these nanocapsules, and genipin was used as a crosslinking agent that increased the drug loading ratio and improved the release time.

Justin and co-workers [81]) synthesized chitosan–rGO nanocomposites through a biocompatible reduction process for application in drug delivery systems, with components being held together by hydrogen bonding. The mechanical and electrical properties of chitosan were improved by adding rGO. The nanocomposites also displayed pH-dependent, quick, and substantial drug release behavior, which accommodated a controlled drug release rate.

The preparation of nanofibers using polyvinyl alcohol and chitosan was reported by Sedghi and coworkers applying the electrospinning technique [82]. The polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) was the linkage agent which enhanced the biomedical properties of the hybrid. A crosslinking agent (Si–O–Si network), PVA, and chitosan, along with functionalization of GO, were employed to generate a novel delivery vehicle via an ionic interaction. The curcumin as a natural anticancer and antibiotic drug was loaded into the nanofibers; this nanocomposite exhibited good drug release behavior. In vitro cell toxicity experiments were performed on MCF-7, HEP G2, and L929 cell lines, and excellent results were obtained.

Bao and coworkers [83] prepared a nanocomposite through a covalent interaction of GO and chitosan via a facile amidation process. This composite possessed good aqueous solubility and biocompatibility and superior loading capacity. The cytotoxicity of the nanocomposite was evaluated in HepG2 and HeLa cell lines.

Fullerenes with various carbon atoms are zero-dimensional (0D) materials with different sizes and shapes. The most known structure of fullerene contains 60 carbon atoms and is recognized as the C60 molecule or buckyball. In these materials, carbon atoms are formed with 12 pentagonal and 20 hexagonal rings linked together; other fullerene structures are C70, C72, C76, C84, and C100. The first large-scale reported synthesis of fullerenes was presented by W. Krätschmer and D. R. Huffman in 1990 by producing graphite electrodes arc in an inert environment [84]) Subsequently, various fullerenes production strategies were reported, including evaporation of graphite in an inert gas [85] unsaturated hydrocarbon combustion [86], sunlight irradiation on graphite [87], and graphite laser ablation [88,89]. The ensuing products contained fullerenes and some impurities; therefore, several purification technologies have been explored to attain pure fullerenes [90–92].

A wide range of molecules can be easily attached to external and internal surfaces of fullerenes thanks their unique structure containing numerous conjugated double bonds. This renders them appropriate candidates for biomedical applications [93–101]. Nevertheless, the hydrophobic properties of fullerenes limit their bio-applications. To circumvent this problem, chemical functionalization with organic compounds such as amino acids, carboxylic acids, polyhydroxy groups, and amphiphilic polymers have been undertaken [102–106]. These can be deployed as a special carrier of therapeutic agents and genes [107–111].

Sukhodub and co-workers [112] reported bioactive composite synthesis by crosslinking chitosan macromolecules with sodium tripolyphosphate. They were then immobilized on inorganic particles containing hydroxyapatite and fullerenes C60 under microwave irradiation conditions. The antimicrobial effect of the synthesized nanocomposites was investigated against S. aureus ATCC 25923 and E. coli ATCC 25922.

Maleki and co-workers [113] found that fullerene functionalized with trimethyl chitosan by electrostatic and Van der Waals interactions can decrease the side effects of some drugs such as Doxorubicin (DOX) and Paclitaxel (PAX) thus improving their biocompatibility. They applied the molecular dynamic (MD) simulations to study the pH-controlled release of these anti-cancer drugs; the highest binding energy made the chitosan functionalized fullerene an ideal drug carrier.

Kwag and co-workers [114] reported a glycol chitosan-grafted fullerene nanocomposite by chemical conjugation of CC double bonds in C60 to free amino groups of glycol chitosan. The chitosan grafting process was developed to enable the solubility of C60 so that it can be used in photodynamic therapy, ablation of tumor cells, and carrying in the body. They indicated that these types of nanocomposites are promising for a photodynamic antitumor therapy for in vivo tumors in nude mice.

Tao and coworkers [115] synthesized a nanocomposite containing GO and folate coupled chitosan loaded with polyprenol and fullerene via noncovalent interactions. This nanocomposite exhibited efficient drug loading and encapsulated materials with good drug release properties and storage stability.

One of the recently discovered carbon allotropes is nanodiamond (ND). NDs are used in various field namely in electromechanical and biomedical applications or chemical reactions, [116]. In addition to their exceptional optical and chemical properties, the easy functionalization of their surface makes them ideal candidates for biomedical applications [117–119]. Moreover, the biocompatibility and nontoxicity of NDs have been demonstrated in numerous studies in recent years; generally, they are considered the safest nanocarbon materials. Therefore, extensive investigations have been conducted into their applicability as drug-delivery agents, diagnostic tools, and imaging implements.

Due to the wide range of applications, commercial ND synthesis methods have been examined. Available synthesis strategies include various carbon precursors and different techniques such as explosion, laser ablation, ultra-sound cavitation, chemical vapor deposition, etc. [120–126]. For bio-applications, further processing of raw forms of NDs attaining a high degree of purity is necessary. Therefore, practical purification methods were devised, e.g., deploying strong liquid oxidizers [127], along with more unconventional purification technologies such as ion-exchange procedures, microwave-assisted techniques, ozone treatment, and high-temperature annealing [128,129].

Further modification is essential for avoiding ND's aggregation and for using this material in therapeutic applications that are divided into two groups: doping and surface functionalization. The doping process led to internal defects by foreign atoms via high-energy techniques that created the optically active compounds [130–135]. Surface functionalization of NDs entailed a chemical reaction with organic compounds such as natural polymers, genetic material peptides, and amino acids in order to improve their biomedical properties [136].

Mahdavi and coworkers [137] successfully generated chitosan-based biopolymers and medical-grade nanodiamonds through electrospinning and investigated their chemical and physical properties. Ali and coworkers [138] employed chitosan for the functionalization of a NDs’ surface to improve its steric stabilization and confer adhesiveness. Doxorubicin (DOX) was loaded onto this nanocomposite and the effect of chitosan molecular weight and pH on its operation was examined. The results revealed high drug loading efficiency, small size, good stability, and acid-favored drug release. The modification of the surface of pristine ND, N, O-carboxymethyl chitosan was employed for protein drug delivery applications. The superior cytotoxicity of the nanocomposite against the HT-1197 cancer cell lines was demonstrated in the MTT assay.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) enable interaction with other molecules due to their various functional groups such as amino, hydroxy, and acidic [139]. Moreover, their advantageous properties, including photo-stability, biocompatibility, water solubility, and low toxicity, make them applicable in drug delivery [140–144]. In this regard, the synthesis of novel and efficient nanocomposites encompassing chitosan, CQDs, and aptamers was reported in 2020 [145]); aptamers are single-stranded DNA or RNA oligonucleotides for selective targeting [146].

Justin and co-workers [147] reported a graphene quantum dot (GQD) preparation strategy through the hydrothermal reduction of an aqueous suspension of GO. Such synthesized compounds had a diameter of 50_55 nm and a height of ∼1.5 nm, and in comparison, with graphene, they displayed more photoluminescent properties and lower toxicity. This research group reported the formation of chitosan-GQD nanocomposites with wonderful properties; their electrical conductivity was 7.9 times higher than that of chitosan with a similar biodegradation rate. They investigated the loading of large molecular weight drugs like bovine serum albumin on the nanocomposite and its release under electrical stimulation.

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are functional graphene-like structures discovered recently [148]; Halder et al., 2018; Hamilton et al., 2011; Kumawat et al., 2017 [8]; Lin et al., 2012). Recent greener procedures for the production of quantum dots, their biomedical and biotechnological applications [19–21,21] and the developmental approaches deployed for the preparation of bioderived NCQDs were compressively reviewed by Varma and co-workers [144]. The conventional stepwise synthesis via bottom-up or top-down methods was applied for the synthesis of GQDs. They possessed photoluminescence properties and were modified according to their surface and edge chemistry. Also, the biocompatibility and low toxicity of GQDs have attracted special attention (Zavareh et al., 2020; [146–149].

Sheng and coworkers [150], prepared carboxyl-functionalized GQDs through pyrolysis of citric acid, the hydrophilic cytarabine, and an anti-cancer drug was loaded onto their surface through the amidation reaction between the amino groups of cytarabine and carboxylic groups of GQDs. Finally, chitosan was used to encapsulate the complexity of drugs and GQDs. The hydrolysis of the amide linkage between the GQDs and cytarabine in an acidic medium made it an efficient pH-sensitive carrier.

Wang and coworkers [147] functionalized carbon dots using polyethylene glycol (PEG)−chitosan for fluorescence imaging and drug release. The fluorescent carbon dots mixed with PEG−chitosan reacted together through the polymerization precipitation process. The DOX was loaded onto the synthesized nanocomposite via simple complexation reactions, NIR light irradiation was applied to control the drug release profile. The in vivo efficiency of the nanocomposite was investigated on kidneys and livers of mice, and the results indicated its capability.
The research into cytotoxicity of carbon nanomaterials is reviewed here, including the diverse structures of carbon nanomaterials with potential contributing factors.
Single-walled carbon nanotubes: Acid-functionalized SWCNTs exhibited cytotoxicity by disrupting mitochondrial and phagocytic activity, which increased with enhancing concentration [151,152].

Multiwalled carbon nanotubes: In the case of MWCNT-induced cytotoxicity, the available data are contradictory. In one report, the toxic properties of two diverse MWCNTs were investigated, with both being found nontoxic to RAW264.7 cells lines [153], while in another report, functionalized MWCNTs led to important cell apoptosis [154]. Diverse types of macrophages specifically respond to MWCNTs. For example, in BMDCs (bone marrow-derived dendritic cells) MWCNTs show higher toxicity even at low concentrations, but for RAW264.7 cells, no cytotoxicity was observed in higher amounts [155].
Fullerene: As expected, fullerene (C60) did not show any toxicity in macrophages [156,157].
Nano graphite: Pure graphene showed cell death through apoptosis and necrosis [158]. According to a competition study, nano graphite led to more cellular toxicity than CNTs [159–161].
Single-walled carbon nanohorns: Several studies indicated their cytotoxicity through necrotic and apoptotic cell death mechanisms (Tahara et al., 2012) (see Scheme 3).

The cytotoxicity of carbon nanomaterials follows various mechanisms as revealed by several scientific efforts; The summary form is schematically depicted in Scheme 4
.
Various physical properties influence the cytotoxicity of carbon-based nanomaterials which includes the following.

Length: The available studies indicated the critical effect of the length of MWCNTs, long ones (20 μm), which increased the generation of reactive oxygen species in human macrophages and led to cytotoxicity, while shorter MWCNTs (0.6 μm) did not exhibit toxicity [162,163]. The influence of the length of CNTs was studied on human acute monocytic leukemia cell lines (THP-1) by Sato et al. where they reported that long CNTs showed higher toxicity than the shorter ones (Sato et al., 2005). In another investigation on NR8383 rat alveolar macrophages, it was found that long CNTs displayed extreme biological activity [164].

Size: Another important parameter of the cytotoxicity of carbon nanomaterials is their size. Scientific studies on MWCNTs revealed some contradictory results. For instance, one study reported that MWCNTs with a diameter more than 50 nm were more toxic to RAW264.7 cells than small-diameter samples [165] while another report indicated that MWCNTs with low diameter exhibited more toxicity due to their enhanced ability to penetrate the cell membrane [166]. The toxicity of two MWCNTs samples with diverse diameters 9.6 nm and 70 nm was examined and the outcomes evidenced that the thinner sample was considerably more toxic than the thicker one [167].

Shape: The geometric structures of carbon nanomaterials have a significant effect on their toxicity. For example, the cytotoxicity of SWCNTs was found to be greater than that of MWCNTs whereas in C60 group compounds, no toxicity was observed. The toxicity of acid-functionalized graphene oxides and SWCNTs was evaluated. The results showed that graphene oxides are significantly more toxic than SWCNTs. These results were rationalized on the basis of their distinct physical features, graphene oxides with flat shapes have more contact with the cell membrane [152]. More studies showed that elongated shape materials like MWCNTs were more toxic than spherical shape compounds, the toxicity order of the nanomaterials was found to be as follows: fullerenes < carbon black nanoparticles < MWCNTs < SWCNTs < graphene.

Surface functionalization: The effect of surface functionalization on their toxicity was investigated by comparing various functional groups for their toxic activity. For instance, the toxicity of non-covalent functionalized MWCNTs by polyethylene glycol (PEG) was compared with the toxicity of carboxylated MWCNTs; bulkier compounds due to their low cellular uptake properties displayed low toxicity. The type of a functional group can alter the cellar response [168]. Within another investigation, the toxicity of pure MWCNTs was compared with their acid-functionalized samples, with the former displaying lower toxicity [169]. The cytotoxicity of protein functionalized CNTs was evaluated in human macrophages, wherein various factors can affect their toxicity behavior such as the nature of the functional protein and the degree of functionalization; some proteins increase CNTs toxicity even at low concentrations and as expected, a high amount of loaded protein caused higher toxicity [170,171]. Generally, the modification of CNTs with water-soluble compounds reduced their toxicity, which is related to the degree of functionalization a greater number of water-soluble materials led to lower toxicity [172–174].

Metal impurities: Due to applications of metals in the preparation process of CNTs, most of them contain metal impurities that can function as oxidizers and lead to toxicity; therefore, metal impurities play a serious role as they cause significantly higher toxicity than in metal-free and pure samples [175,176]. According to available reports, the synthesis procedures and the purification process of CNTs influence the toxicity properties of the final compounds; for instance, the acid purification process causes more toxic compounds [177]. Having reviewed the toxicity of carbon nanomaterials, available findings are controversial and do not reveal a clear picture for various reasons.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Strategies and challenges for the commercial development of nanopharmaceuticals
ce:section.ce:para: The commercialization of nanopharmaceuticals is rather different and complicated in terms of the process development and the level of investment is relative to other products emanating from nanotechnology (Scheme 5 ). Although, studies on the nano-drug delivery system have recently become a major academic effort leading to advancement in this field, comprehending the challenges and approaches for commercial development will be important for the future [178–180].

One of the important challenges is the possibility of large-scale production of drug delivery systems following Good Manufacturing Practices (GMP) conditions. The first step of attaining an industrial product often entails academic lab-scale synthesis dealing with milligrams, while for clinical trials, larger quantities are needed. Large-scale production is a challenging matter because the properties of the final product may alter with minor changes in procedure. Moreover, the building of nano-drug delivery systems usually comprises multi-step processes, which complicate the industrial scale adaption. Therefore, in designing a nano-based industrial drug system, balancing the ease and complexity of synthesis is required [181–183].

Another challenge regarding commercializing nano-based drug delivery systems is the lack of supplies for regulatory agencies (FDA and EMA) in their clinical testing, and up to now, the evaluation process is similar to the one that is deployed for small molecule drugs, as presented in Scheme 6 . The most important step in the preclinical evaluation is the investigation of the products’ toxicity and their reasonable therapeutic efficacy; with good results, the products will continue to develop through in vitro and in vivo tests. After indicating the clinical trial application, the clinical use of nano-drug delivery systems will be explored through a similar path as has been followed for the small molecules; clinical trials involve three phases of evaluation of the safety and therapeutic usefulness [184].

Despite numerous scientific reports and academic attainments, only a small number of nanocarbon-based drug delivery systems have been formulated as clinical cases because of the aforementioned problems. The production processes for most of nanocarbon-based materials entail some disadvantages (Li et al., 2002). However, the extensive market of nanopharmaceuticals comprising more than 250 commercialized nanoproducts and thus occupying more than 15% of the total market, is a threshold for creating a new generation of commercial nanopharmaceuticals of the future.
